personally.definition: Speaking about yourself; from your own experience.
personally.example: Personally, I found it hard at first.

reciprocity.definition: A situation where two people give and receive equally.
reciprocity.example: Reciprocity is important in any relationship.

unconscious.definition: Not aware; happening without you knowing.
unconscious.example: We often follow unconscious rules from childhood.

belief.example: Her belief was that children should be quiet.

adapt.definition: To change to fit a new situation.
adapt.example: Parents must adapt to their child's needs.

resistance.definition: The act of refusing to accept or do something.
resistance.example: She noticed some resistance to letting her child lead.

toddler.definition: A young child who is just learning to walk, usually 1-3 years old.
toddler.example: The toddler ran around the room.

diaphobia.definition: A fear of real dialogue; fear of being affected by others.
diaphobia.example: Diaphobia can prevent parents from truly connecting.

phobia.definition: A very strong fear of something.
phobia.example: A phobia can stop you from doing normal things.

impacted.example: She was impacted by her child's sadness.

tend.definition: To usually do something; to have a habit.
tend.example: We tend to repeat patterns from our parents.

innate.definition: Something you are born with; natural.
innate.example: Babies have an innate ability to bond.

deadened.definition: Made less strong or less sensitive.
deadened.example: His ability to feel was deadened by years of neglect.

practical.definition: Related to real actions and daily life, not just ideas.
practical.example: She provided practical help like cooking and cleaning.

infancy.definition: The time when someone is a baby.

individual.definition: A single person, separate from others.
individual.example: Every child is a unique individual.

fostering.definition: Encouraging or helping something to develop.
fostering.example: Not responding to a child risks fostering insecurity.

insecure.chinese: 不安全的；沒有安全感的
insecure.example: An insecure child may cling to their parent.

avoidant.definition: Trying to stay away from closeness or connection.
avoidant.example: An avoidant child may not seek comfort from parents.

attachment.definition: A strong emotional connection between people.
attachment.example: Secure attachment helps children feel safe.

personality.definition: The qualities that make a person different from others.
personality.example: Her personality is warm and kind.

traits.definition: Qualities or characteristics of a person.
traits.example: Kindness and patience are good traits.

functional.definition: Working well; able to operate normally.
functional.example: Children need functional relationships to grow.

admonish.definition: To scold or warn someone about their behavior.
admonish.example: Don't admonish yourself for past mistakes.

blame.definition: To say that someone is responsible for something bad.
blame.example: Don't blame yourself for not knowing better.

ashamed.definition: Feeling bad or embarrassed about something you did.
ashamed.example: She felt ashamed of her reaction.

detected.example: She detected a pattern in her behavior.

shying.definition: Moving away from something because of fear or nervousness.
shying.example: She was shying away from close contact.

teenager.definition: A young person between 13 and 19 years old.
teenager.example: Her teenager still needed her attention.

regretful.definition: Feeling sorry about something that happened.
regretful.example: She had regretful feelings about the past.

pangs.definition: Sudden sharp feelings of pain or emotion.
pangs.example: She felt pangs of guilt about her parenting.

diaphobic.definition: Having a fear of real dialogue or being affected by others.
diaphobic.example: A diaphobic parent avoids true connection.

perspective.definition: A way of looking at something; a point of view.
perspective.example: Try to see things from your child's perspective.

surrender.example: I'm not saying you should surrender your opinions.

defer.definition: To let someone else decide; to give way to another person.
defer.example: You don't need to defer to the child on everything.

equally.definition: In the same way or to the same degree.

sarcastically.definition: In a way that means the opposite of what you say, often to be hurtful.

muddle.definition: A state of confusion or mess.
muddle.example: He was getting in a muddle with his pills.

medication.definition: Medicine; drugs used to treat illness.
medication.example: He needed help organizing his medication.

chart.definition: A diagram or table that shows information clearly.
chart.example: She drew up a chart for the daily schedule.

labels.definition: Words or information written on something to describe it.
labels.example: Read the labels on the medicine bottles.

hurtful.example: His words were hurtful, even if he didn't mean them.

appropriate.definition: Right or suitable for the situation.
appropriate.example: A thank you would have been more appropriate.

attitude.definition: The way you think and feel about something.
attitude.example: His attitude towards being told things changed.

patronizing.definition: Treating someone as if they are less important or less intelligent.
patronizing.example: Don't be patronizing when your child shows you something.

improving.example: Their relationship is improving every day.

curiosity.definition: A strong desire to know or learn.
curiosity.example: Children are full of curiosity about the world.

crush.definition: To destroy or damage badly; to press something so hard it breaks.
crush.example: Don't let shame crush your curiosity.

opposite.example: The result was the opposite of what I expected.

consequences.definition: Results or effects of an action.

behaviour.example: Small changes in behaviour can make a big difference.

benefits.example: Listening to your child has many benefits.

chores.definition: Regular tasks that need to be done at home.
chores.example: She used chores as an excuse to avoid the baby.

urgent.definition: Needing to be done right away.
urgent.example: Nothing is more urgent than your child's needs.

excuse.definition: A reason you give to explain why you did or did not do something.
excuse.example: Don't use work as an excuse to push them away.

override.definition: To ignore or go against a natural reaction.
override.example: Override the instinct to push your child away.

instinct.definition: A natural feeling or reaction that you don't think about.
instinct.example: Trust your instinct as a parent.

trace.definition: To find the beginning or cause of something.
trace.example: Can you trace this feeling back to your childhood?

origins.definition: Where something began or came from.
origins.example: The origins of her fear were in childhood.

constant.definition: Happening all the time without stopping.
constant.example: You don't need constant face-to-face contact.

ignore.definition: To not pay attention to something or someone.
ignore.example: If you ignore your baby's cries, they feel alone.

bids.definition: Attempts to get someone's attention or response.
bids.example: Children make many bids for their parents' attention.

distressing.definition: Causing great worry, sadness, or pain.
distressing.example: Being ignored is distressing for a baby.

experiment.definition: A test done to find out what happens.
experiment.example: The experiment showed how babies react to silence.

instructed.example: The mothers were instructed to show no expression.

mimicking.example: Babies learn by mimicking their parents.

gesturing.definition: Making movements with hands or face to show meaning.
gesturing.example: The mother stopped gesturing to her baby.

responsiveness.definition: The ability to react quickly and positively to someone.
responsiveness.example: Emotional responsiveness is key for babies.

distress.example: The baby showed signs of distress.

anxiety.definition: A feeling of worry or fear.
anxiety.example: The baby showed anxiety when ignored.

shame.definition: A painful feeling of embarrassment or not being good enough.

lingered.definition: Stayed for a longer time than expected.
lingered.example: The sadness lingered for several minutes.

caregivers.definition: People who take care of a child or someone who needs help.
caregivers.example: Caregivers play an important role in a child's life.

helplessness.definition: The feeling that you cannot do anything to change your situation.
helplessness.example: Children learn helplessness when they are ignored.

accidentally.example: We can accidentally teach bad habits.

cliché.definition: A phrase or idea that has been used too much and lost its meaning.
cliché.example: The classic man cliché is not asking for directions.

directions.definition: Instructions on how to get somewhere.
directions.example: He learned to ask for directions without feeling shame.

classic.definition: A typical or well-known example of something.
classic.example: It was the classic mistake of talking too much.

pepper.definition: To fill or sprinkle with many of something.
pepper.example: He would pepper his sentences with 'I know.'

catchphrase.definition: A word or phrase that someone uses very often.

apparently.definition: It seems; based on what others say.
apparently.example: Apparently, he says it without realizing.
